criteria: The agent has read Section 4 (Proposed Method) completely to understand the Token Prepending technique: prepending each layer's decoded sentence embedding to the beginning of the sentence in the next layer's input.

The agent has read Section 4.2.1 to understand Initial Token Prepending: a custom <PST> token (not in LLM vocabulary) is randomly initialized and prepended to the input text in the embedding layer output.

The agent has read Section 4.2.2 to understand Intermediate Token Prepending: at each layer l in [2, k], the <PST> token embedding is replaced by the hidden state of the last token (SET) from the previous layer before feeding into the next Transformer layer.
The agent has read Section 4.2.3 to understand the layer scope for TP: after layer k, TP stops and standard forward propagation resumes through layer M (the exit layer).

The agent has read Section 4.3 to understand the early-exit strategy: embeddings from an intermediate layer M (determined using STS-B validation set) are used as sentence embeddings instead of the final layer.

The agent has read Section 5.1 to check the default hyperparameters: TP stops after the 8th layer (k=8), exit at the 27th layer for PromptEOL/MetaEOL/Pretended CoT, and penultimate layer for Knowledge Enhancement; <PST> is placed before [Text] in all prompts.

The agent has read Table 1 to check the main STS results using LLaMA2-7B: PromptEOL+TP achieves 77.19 avg (up 7.16), MetaEOL+TP achieves 77.91 avg (up 1.95), and the inference time column showing TP adds at most 1.04x overhead.
The agent has read Table 2 to understand the backbone comparison: Pretended CoT+TP is evaluated on LLaMA2-7B, LLaMA2-13B, LLaMA3-8B, Qwen2-7B, and Gemma2-9B, showing consistent improvements.

The agent has read Table 3 to understand the <PST> token position analysis: placing <PST> after the text is worst, placing before the text yields stable performance, and the default is placing it after the colon.

The agent has read Figure 3 to understand the ablation of <PST> token before and after intermediate TP: removing <PST> after intermediate TP has a more pronounced negative impact than removing it before.

The agent has read Figure 4 to understand the layer scope analysis: (a) start layer should be 2, (b) end layer k=8 is optimal for LLaMA2-7B, (c) k=7 is optimal for Qwen2-7B and Gemma2-9B, and (d) the last layer is consistently suboptimal for exit.

The agent has read Table 5 to understand the transfer learning evaluation: PromptEOL+TP achieves 91.42 avg accuracy on 7 SentEval tasks (MR, CR, SUBJ, MPQA, SST-2, TREC, MRPC), outperforming baseline in 20/21 cases.

The agent has read Figure 5 and Section 5.9 to understand the dependency-capturing analysis: Spearman correlation between the last (pivot) token and all other tokens is computed per sentence, with LLaMA2+TP scoring 25.11 vs 23.97 for vanilla LLaMA2.

The agent has read Appendix A.1 to understand the bidirectional attention comparison: both bidirectional attention mask variants (last token only and full input sentence) substantially decrease STS performance compared to TP.

The agent has read Appendix A.2 to understand the multi-task MTEB evaluation: PromptEOL+TP is evaluated on 12 classification, 3 pair classification, 4 reranking, 11 clustering, and 1 summarization datasets.

The agent has read Appendix A.3 to understand the additional prompt evaluation: Prompts A and B (simple prompts similar to PromptEOL) benefit more from TP (+10.64 and +9.26), while Prompts C and D with explicit PST semantics do not further improve TP.

The agent has read Appendix A.4 through A.7 to understand the ablation studies on number of <PST> tokens, masking the <PST> in the first layer, resuming TP at deeper layers, and TP without any prompt.
The plan includes the overall Token Prepending mechanism: a layer-level intervention that prepends sentence embeddings to the beginning of each layer's input within a specified layer range.
The plan includes adding a new custom <PST> token to the LLM's tokenizer and embedding layer with random initialization.
The plan includes the intermediate TP hook: at each layer l in [2, k], replace the <PST> token position in the hidden states with the hidden state of the last token (the SET) from that same layer.
The plan includes two tunable hyperparameters: k (the layer at which TP stops) and M (the exit layer from which sentence embeddings are extracted), both determined via STS-B dev set.

The plan includes the default TP configuration: k=8 for LLaMA2-7B (k=7 for Qwen2-7B and Gemma2-9B), exit at layer 27 for PromptEOL/MetaEOL/Pretended CoT, and penultimate layer for Knowledge Enhancement.
The plan includes evaluation on 7 STS datasets (STS12, STS13, STS14, STS15, STS16, STS-B, SICK-R) using cosine similarity to compute predicted similarity and Spearman correlation as the metric.
The plan includes implementation of all four prompt-based baselines: PromptEOL, MetaEOL, Pretended CoT, and Knowledge Enhancement.
The plan includes evaluating TP across multiple backbone models: LLaMA2-7B, LLaMA2-13B, LLaMA3-8B, Qwen2-7B, and Gemma2-9B.
The plan includes the transfer learning evaluation using SentEval: extracting sentence embeddings and training logistic regression classifiers on MR, CR, SUBJ, MPQA, SST-2, TREC, and MRPC tasks.
The plan includes the layer scope analysis experiments: varying the start layer, end layer k, and exit layer M for intermediate TP across multiple prompt methods.
The plan includes the <PST> token position ablation: testing different positions in the prompt (before text, after text, at different spots before the colon).
The plan includes the <PST> token initialization experiment: comparing all-0, all-1, uniform, Gaussian, and existing token (space character) initialization methods.
The plan includes the multi-task MTEB evaluation across classification, pair classification, reranking, clustering, and summarization datasets using PromptEOL and PromptEOL+TP.
The code for adding a new special <PST> token to the LLM's tokenizer vocabulary has been implemented.
The code for randomly initializing the embedding vector for the <PST> token and incorporating it into the model's embedding layer has been implemented.
The code for inserting the <PST> token at the correct position in the prompt template (placed before [Text], after the colon by default) has been implemented.

The code for Initial Token Prepending has been implemented: the embedding layer output is modified to h^0 = [h_1^0, ..., h_{i-1}^0, h_{i*}^0, h_i^0, ..., h_n^0] where h_{i*}^0 is the randomly initialized <PST> embedding.

The code for the Intermediate Token Prepending function f(h) has been implemented: within layers [2, k], the hidden state at the <PST> position is replaced by the hidden state h_n^{l-1} of the last token (SET) from the previous layer.
The code correctly applies TP only in the layer range [2, k] and reverts to standard Transformer forward propagation h^{l+1} = LLM^l(h^l) for layers l in [k, M] as specified in Equation 4.
The code for the early-exit strategy has been implemented: extracting the hidden state of the SET (last token) at layer M rather than the final layer L as the output sentence embedding.
The code for the PromptEOL prompt template has been implemented: 'This sentence: "[Text]" means in one word: "' with the last token used to decode the sentence embedding.
The code for the MetaEOL baseline using diverse meta-task prompts generated by ChatGPT-4 has been implemented.
The code for the Pretended CoT and Knowledge Enhancement prompt-based baselines has been implemented.
The code for the Echo Embeddings baseline (LLaMA2 echo) has been implemented: repeating the input sentence twice and extracting embeddings from the second occurrence.
The code for loading and evaluating all 7 STS datasets (STS12, STS13, STS14, STS15, STS16, STS-B, SICK-R) has been implemented.
The code for computing cosine similarity between sentence embedding pairs and Spearman correlation against annotated similarity scores (0-5 scale) has been implemented.
The code for evaluating TP across multiple backbone models (LLaMA2-7B, LLaMA2-13B, LLaMA3-8B, Qwen2-7B, Gemma2-9B) has been implemented, correctly configuring optimal k and M for each model.
The code for using KV cache during inference to mitigate the overhead of repeatedly loading the prompt prefix has been implemented.
The code for inference time measurement (batch size=1 on STS-B test dataset with the same output layer across methods) has been implemented.

The code for the transfer learning evaluation has been implemented: extracting sentence embeddings using LLaMA2-7B and training logistic regression classifiers on SentEval tasks (MR, CR, SUBJ, MPQA, SST-2, TREC, MRPC).

The code for the dependency-capturing analysis has been implemented: computing per-sentence Spearman correlation between the last (pivot) token's hidden state and all other tokens' hidden states on the STS-B test set.

The code for the <PST> token position experiment has been implemented: testing multiple positions of the <PST> token in the prompt template (before text at colon, before text with space, after text) for both PromptEOL and Pretended CoT.

The code for the <PST> token initialization experiment has been implemented: testing all-0, all-1, uniform distribution in [0,1], Gaussian distribution, and existing token (space character embedding) initialization.

The code for the layer scope analysis experiments has been implemented: sweeping over different start layers (Figure 4a), end layers k (Figure 4b), and exit layers M (Figure 4d) with multiple prompt methods on LLaMA2-7B.
The code for the <PST> token ablation experiment (Figure 3) has been implemented: comparing full TP versus removing <PST> before intermediate TP versus removing <PST> after intermediate TP.

The code for the bidirectional attention comparison (Appendix A.1) has been implemented: testing two bidirectional attention mask variants (last token only, full input sentence) using Pretended CoT on LLaMA2-7B.

The code for the multi-task MTEB evaluation (Appendix A.2) has been implemented: running PromptEOL and PromptEOL+TP across 12 classification, 3 pair classification, 4 reranking, 11 clustering, and 1 summarization datasets from MTEB benchmark.

The code for the additional prompt baseline experiments (Appendix A.3) has been implemented: evaluating TP with Prompt A ('The representative word for sentence <PST> "[TEXT]" is:') and Prompt B ('Summarize sentence <PST> "[TEXT]" in one word:').
The code for the number of <PST> tokens experiment (Appendix A.4) has been implemented: testing 1, 2, 3, and 4 <PST> tokens prepended in the PromptEOL setting.
The code for masking the <PST> token in the first layer (Appendix A.5) has been implemented to test whether the <PST> token's role as a placeholder for input length matters.
The code for the TP resumption experiment (Appendix A.6) has been implemented: applying TP for the first 8 layers, pausing, then resuming at later layers (9, 10, 11, 16, 26) until exit.
The code for TP without any prompt (Appendix A.7) has been implemented: testing TP's standalone contribution without prompt engineering on LLaMA2-7B.

The main STS experiments using LLaMA2-7B with all four prompt methods (PromptEOL, MetaEOL, Pretended CoT, Knowledge Enhancement) both with and without TP have been executed successfully without errors.
The backbone comparison experiments using Pretended CoT across LLaMA2-7B, LLaMA2-13B, LLaMA3-8B, Qwen2-7B, and Gemma2-9B have been executed successfully without errors.
The transfer learning evaluation on 7 SentEval tasks (MR, CR, SUBJ, MPQA, SST-2, TREC, MRPC) using LLaMA2-7B with and without TP has been executed successfully without errors.
The layer scope analysis experiments (varying start layer, end layer k across prompts, end layer k across backbones, and exit layer M) have been executed successfully without errors.
The dependency-capturing analysis on the STS-B test set with LLaMA2-7B using Pretended CoT (both with and without TP) has been executed successfully without errors.
The multi-task MTEB evaluation (Appendix A.2) with PromptEOL and PromptEOL+TP on LLaMA2-7B has been executed successfully without errors across all 32 datasets.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: PromptEOL+TP achieves an average STS Spearman score of 77.19 (PromptEOL baseline: 70.03, improvement of 7.16) on LLaMA2-7B.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: MetaEOL+TP achieves an average STS Spearman score of 77.91 (MetaEOL baseline: 75.96, improvement of 1.95) on LLaMA2-7B.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: Pretended CoT+TP achieves 77.54 (up 0.68) and Knowledge+TP achieves 77.54 (up 0.40) on LLaMA2-7B, showing consistent but smaller gains for knowledge-enhanced prompts.
The execution outcomes match the Time column of Table 1: TP adds at most 1.04x inference time relative to PromptEOL (batch size 1, same output layer, with KV cache).

The execution outcomes match Table 2: Pretended CoT+TP outperforms Pretended CoT on all five backbone models, with the largest improvement of 2.17 on Qwen2-7B and smallest improvement of 0.50 on Gemma2-9B.
The execution outcomes match Table 5: PromptEOL+TP achieves 91.42 average accuracy on transfer tasks (up 0.48 from 90.94) and improves in 20 out of 21 cases across all 7 SentEval datasets.

The execution outcomes match Figure 4(a): for PromptEOL, performance degrades notably when intermediate TP starts at layer 5 or later (score drops to ~66), confirming the necessity of starting TP from layer 2.
The execution outcomes match Figure 4(b): ending TP at the 8th layer yields the best performance for all four prompt methods on LLaMA2-7B, with scores peaking at k=8.

The execution outcomes match Figure 4(c): LLaMA2-7B and LLaMA2-13B peak at k=8 while Qwen2-7B and Gemma2-9B peak at k=7, confirming that the optimal end layer is typically in the 7th-8th range for ~7B parameter LLMs.

The execution outcomes match Figure 4(d): the last layer (layer 32) consistently yields the worst STS performance for both Pretended CoT and Knowledge Enhancement, confirming the benefit of early-exit; Pretended CoT peaks at layer 27 and Knowledge Enhancement at layer 31 (penultimate).

The execution outcomes match Table 3: placing <PST> after the input text (e.g., 'This sentence: "[Text]" <PST> means in one word:') yields the worst performance, while placing it before the text at the default position yields the best results.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: the initialization method of the <PST> token has minimal impact on STS performance, with maximum difference of only 0.01 across all-0, all-1, uniform, Gaussian, and existing token methods.
The execution outcomes match Figure 3: ablating <PST> after intermediate TP has a larger performance drop than ablating it before intermediate TP across all prompt methods.

The execution outcomes match Figure 5: LLaMA2-7B+TP achieves a higher average sentence-level Spearman correlation of 25.11 versus 23.97 for vanilla LLaMA2-7B, confirming improved backward dependency capturing.

The execution outcomes match Table 6: both bidirectional attention variants substantially decrease STS performance (53.06 and 43.70) compared to TP (77.54) and even the vanilla baseline (76.86), confirming TP's advantage over attention mask modifications.

The execution outcomes match Tables 7-12 (Appendix A.2): PromptEOL+TP improves PromptEOL in 40 out of 44 cases across MTEB tasks, with the largest gains in clustering (+4.51 avg) and pair classification (+4.21 avg).

The execution outcomes match Table 13 (Appendix A.3): Prompt A+TP improves Prompt A by 10.64 points (53.86 to 64.50) and Prompt B+TP improves Prompt B by 9.26 points (65.52 to 74.78), confirming that simpler prompts benefit more from TP.
The execution outcomes match Table 14 (Appendix A.4): using 2 <PST> tokens yields a slight improvement of 0.08 over 1 <PST> token (77.27 vs 77.19), while 3 and 4 tokens lead to performance decline.
